description: The classic Nigerian breakfast and quick meal — boiled yam with a rich, savoury tomato and egg sauce cooked in onions, pepper, and seasoning. Simple, fast, and deeply satisfying when done right.

ingredients.name: white yam (puna yam)
ingredients.note: Puna yam is the standard for boiling. Avoid water yam — it is too watery and sticky for this. Peel thickly — the outer layer just under the skin is often fibrous.

ingredients.name: eggs
ingredients.note: Room temperature eggs cook more evenly. Do not beat until just before adding to the pan.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Blend for a smoother sauce or dice finely for a chunkier texture. Both are valid — personal preference.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: Adds sweetness and colour to the sauce. Optional but recommended.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: 1 gives background heat without being aggressive. Skip for a completely mild sauce.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 into the tomato blend, 1 sliced for frying in the sauce. The fried onion gives the sauce its base sweetness.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: For frying the sauce base. Do not use too much — the sauce should not be oily.

ingredients.name: seasoning cube
ingredients.note: Crush before adding to the sauce.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the yam water and the egg sauce separately.

ingredients.name: spring onions (scallions)
ingredients.note: Slice and add at the end for freshness and colour.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: A tiny amount adds warmth and colour to the egg sauce. Optional.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.note: For boiling the yam. Enough to fully submerge the yam pieces.

steps.title: Peel and Boil the Yam
steps.instruction: Peel the yam with a sharp knife — peel thickly, removing the outer skin and the layer just beneath it which is often fibrous and slightly bitter. Slice into rounds or wedges about 3–4cm thick. Rinse under cold water. Place in a pot, cover with cold salted water. Bring to a boil over high heat then reduce to medium. Cook until a fork passes through the thickest piece with no resistance — the yam should feel buttery and offer no pushback.
steps.sensory_cue: Properly boiled yam is creamy-white, slightly fluffy at the edges, and smells faintly earthy and sweet. A fork inserted should slide in and out with zero resistance. The cooking water will be slightly starchy and cloudy.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked yam is starchy, waxy, and difficult to eat — and it tastes raw. Test multiple pieces including the thickest one before draining. Do not rush this step.

steps.title: Blend the Pepper Base
steps.instruction: While the yam cooks, blend the tomatoes, half the onion, tatashe, and scotch bonnet together until smooth. If you prefer a chunky sauce, dice the tomatoes finely and skip blending. Slice the remaining onion into thin rings or half-moons.
steps.sensory_cue: Smooth, vibrant red-orange blend. It should smell fresh, sharp, and slightly peppery. No chunks if blended.

steps.title: Fry the Sauce Base
steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onion — fry until soft and translucent, about 3 minutes. Pour in the blended tomato and pepper mix. Fry, stirring regularly, until the liquid reduces and the oil begins to float to the surface — about 8–10 minutes. The raw tomato smell should be completely gone. Add curry powder if using.
steps.sensory_cue: The tomato base should look reduced, slightly darker, and the oil should be clearly visible floating on top. The raw, sharp tomato smell should have transformed into a rich, cooked, slightly sweet aroma.
steps.failure_risk: Adding eggs to an underfried tomato base gives a sour, raw-tasting egg sauce. The base must be fully cooked and reduced before the eggs go in.

steps.title: Add Seasoning
steps.instruction: Add the crushed seasoning cube to the fried tomato base. Add salt. Stir well and taste — the sauce base should be well-seasoned at this point because the eggs will dilute the flavour slightly when added.
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base should taste bold, savoury, and slightly over-seasoned — this is correct. The eggs will mellow it out.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoning before the eggs go in results in a flat, bland egg sauce that is impossible to fix after the eggs are cooked in.

steps.title: Add the Eggs
steps.instruction: Beat the eggs lightly in a bowl — just enough to break the yolks and combine, not to make them frothy. Reduce heat to medium-low. Pour the beaten eggs evenly over the tomato base. Do not stir immediately — let the eggs set slightly at the bottom and edges for about 30 seconds. Then stir gently, folding the eggs into the sauce in large, slow movements. Stop when the eggs are just set — slightly soft and custardy. Remove from heat immediately.
steps.sensory_cue: The eggs should look soft, custardy, and deeply coloured from the tomato sauce. They should be just set — not dry, not runny. The sauce should coat the eggs. Think of it as eggs scrambled inside a tomato sauce rather than scrambled eggs with tomato added after.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked eggs turn dry, rubbery, and separate from the sauce. Remove from heat the moment they look just set — residual heat will finish them. High heat at this stage is the enemy.

steps.title: Drain Yam and Serve
steps.instruction: Drain the boiled yam immediately once done. Do not leave it sitting in the hot water — it continues cooking and can become waterlogged. Plate the yam pieces and spoon the egg sauce generously over or beside them. Garnish with sliced spring onions if using. Serve immediately — yam and egg sauce does not hold well.
steps.sensory_cue: The plate should be a contrast of white, fluffy yam and the bright orange-red egg sauce with soft yellow egg curds throughout. The yam should steam when broken open. One mouthful of yam with egg sauce together should be creamy, savoury, and comforting.
steps.failure_risk: Yam left in the hot cooking water after it is done becomes waterlogged and breaks apart on the plate. Drain immediately and serve.
